title: Supply-side policy
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS supply-side policy — LRAS shifts, market-based and interventionist measures, and long-run growth with GeoGebra.
learningObjectives: Define supply-side policy and its objectives.
Analyse rightward LRAS shifts on AD–AS diagram.
Evaluate short-run costs and distributional effects.

simpleExplanation.title: Growing productive capacity
simpleExplanation.summary: Supply-side policies aim to increase long-run aggregate supply — raising potential output without demand-pull inflation.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Demand-side policy fills more orders at the shop; supply-side policy builds a bigger factory — you can serve more customers without prices spiralling.
steps: Market-based: lower income tax, deregulation, privatisation.
LRAS shifts right → higher Yf at lower equilibrium price level.
Trade-off: benefits long-run; transitional unemployment possible.

flashcards.back: Policies to increase productive capacity and efficiency — shifting LRAS right to raise potential output (Yf) and reduce cost-push pressure.
Lower income/corporation tax, reduced regulation, privatisation, and trade union reform — aim to increase incentives and competition.
Government investment in education, training, infrastructure, and R&D — aim to improve human capital and productivity.

flashcards.front: LRAS shift right — effect on P and Y?
flashcards.back: Potential output (Yf) rises; in equilibrium, real GDP can grow with lower inflationary pressure — P may fall or rise more slowly.
Demand-side (fiscal/monetary) shifts AD; supply-side shifts LRAS — non-inflationary growth if matched by AD.
Long time lags, upfront costs, possible rise in inequality (tax cuts benefit higher earners), and transitional structural unemployment.

sections.content: **Supply-side policy** (5.4) contrasts with demand-side **fiscal** and **monetary** tools. Show **LRAS shift right** — not just AD — when answering growth and inflation questions together.

items: **Market-based**: tax cuts on enterprise, deregulation, flexible labour markets.
**Both aim**: higher productivity, lower NAIRU, faster potential growth.

sections.question:
An economy has Y = Yf = $600bn and inflation 4%. The government invests $30bn in education and transport over five years, raising productivity.

(a) Show the effect on LRAS.
(b) Predict the effect on Yf, P, and unemployment in the long run.
(c) Compare with a $30bn increase in G on consumer subsidies.
sections.solution:
(a) **LRAS shift**
Draw **LRAS₁** vertical at $600bn. Better skills and infrastructure → **LRAS₂** to the **right** at e.g. $650bn.

(b) **Long-run effects**
**Yf rises** to ~$650bn → economy can grow without hitting capacity constraints → **non-inflationary growth** possible. If AD adjusts gradually, **P pressure eases** (or rises more slowly). **Structural unemployment** falls as workers match new jobs.

(c) **Comparison with $30bn G on subsidies**
Subsidy → **AD shifts right** immediately → at Yf, mainly **P rises** (demand-pull inflation) with limited sustainable Y gain.

Supply-side → **capacity expands** → growth without the same inflation trade-off, but **benefits take years** (time lag).

sections.content:
Supply-side policies can reduce **both unemployment and inflation** in the long run — unlike demand-side trade-offs. However:

- **Time lags** — education and infrastructure take years.
- **Cost** — must be financed (taxes or borrowing).
- **Inequality** — tax cuts may benefit higher earners disproportionately.
- **Transitional unemployment** — privatisation and deregulation may cause job losses before gains appear.
In combined macro essays, use **two diagrams**: one showing AD shift (demand-side) and one showing LRAS shift (supply-side). Examiners reward distinguishing short-run and long-run effects.

steps.caption: Supply-side: shift LRAS right — productive capacity and efficiency.
steps.embedHint: Rightward LRAS — higher Y without demand-pull inflation.

steps.caption: Market-based: tax incentives, deregulation, privatisation.
steps.embedHint: Lower marginal tax on enterprise — incentive channel.

steps.caption: Interventionist: education, training, infrastructure.
steps.embedHint: Human capital ↑ → productivity per worker rises.

steps.caption: Trade-off: benefits long-run; short-run costs and inequality effects.
steps.embedHint: Compare LR growth gain vs transitional unemployment.
